name: Great Britain Street
description: After the 1891 death of Charles Stewart Parnell, a leading Irish Nationalist, the name was changed from "Great Britain" to "Parnell" Street.

name: Canal Bridge
description: It's a bridge crossing the Royal Canal and is generally identified as Newcomen Bridge, in the North Strand area.

name: Pigeon House
description: The Pigeon House was the electricity and power station which served the city of Dublin. It is now a deserted port.

name: North Strand Road
description: North Strand Road is a street in the Northside of Dublin.

name: Wharf Road
description: The Wharf Road, now East Wall Road, is a road that runs along the top of the wall built to keep the River Tolka on course on its way to the sea so that it doesn't flood the city's north-eastern neighbourhoods and industrial complexes.

name: North Richmond Street
description: North Richmond Street is a quiet cul-de-sac on Dublin's north side and forms the opening setting of Araby. Joyce uses it to establish the confined domestic world of the narrator before his journey toward the bazaar begins.

name: Vitriol Works
description: Vitriol (sulphuric acid) was used for bleaching linen cloth from the mid-18th century onward.

name: River Liffey
description: The Liffey is Dublin's main river, cutting through the heart of the city and splitting it into a northern and southern half.
stories: An Encounter|A Little Cloud|A Painful Case

name: Christian Brothers School
description: The Christian Brothers' School on North Richmond Street is mentioned in Araby and also appears in Ivy Day in the Committee Room. It reflects the strong presence of Catholic education in everyday Dublin life during Joyce's time.
stories: Araby|Ivy Day in the Committee Room

name: Buckingham Street
description: Buckingham Street lies on the north side of Dublin and forms part of the boy's route toward the bazaar in Araby. The street helps trace his movement from a quiet residential neighbourhood into the wider city.

name: Westland Row Station
description: Westland Row Station appears in both Araby and After the Race as a point of travel and departure. It represents Dublin's growing modern transport network at the turn of the twentieth century.

name: Naas Road
description: Naas Road forms the western approach into Dublin and is mentioned in After the Race. The racing cars travel along this road as they speed toward the city, symbolising modernity and excitement.

name: Dame Street
description: Dame Street is one of Dublin's major city-centre streets and a hub of commercial activity. In Two Gallants and A Little Cloud it forms part of the busy urban landscape through which the characters move.

name: St Stephen's Green
description: St Stephen's Green is Dublin's best-known public park and a landmark in the city centre. In Two Gallants Lenehan passes through the park during his wanderings across Dublin.

name: Rutland Square
description: Rutland Square is the first location mentioned in Two Gallants. Its Georgian setting reflects the faded elegance of nineteenth-century Dublin. It was renamed Parnell Square in 1933 and features the Garden of Remembrance park.

name: Baggot Street
description: Baggot St. runs southeast from St. Stephen's Green to the Grand Canal.

name: Donnybrook Tram
description: The Donnybrook tram line was launched in 1873, connecting Merrion Square to Donnybrook's southern terminus. The Donnybrook tramway is no longer in operation but is now used as the Donnybrook Bus Garage.

name: Trinity College Dublin
description: Trinity College was founded in 1592 by Elizabeth I. It was built upon the grounds of All Hallows, a former Augustinian priory that had been dissolved during the Reformation.

name: Temple Bar
description: Temple Bar is an area on the south bank of the River Liffey in central Dublin, lined with pubs and restaurants. Farrington moves through this zone on his drinking crawl. Today it's best known as the city's main tourist and nightlife district.

name: Shelbourne Road
description: Shelbourne Road is a "lower-middle-class" residential street in Ballsbridge, connecting Merrion Road toward the Bath Avenue/Haddington Road area. It's where Farrington exits the tram at the end of his exhausting night.

name: Nelson's Pillar
description: Nelson's Pillar stood on O'Connell Street and was a major city-centre landmark and meeting point, shaping how people navigated central Dublin. It was destroyed in 1966.

name: Lucan Road
description: The Lucan Road runs westward along the south side of the Liffey, connecting Chapelizod to Lucan. Its westward direction connotes a departure — an escape from the city.

name: Crowe Street
description: Crowe Street is located in the Temple Bar area south of the River Liffey. In Grace it is the location of Tom Kernan's workplace in the city.

name: Suffolk Street
description: Suffolk Street is a short street connecting the Grafton Street area with St Andrew's Street. In Ivy Day in the Committee Room it serves as a meeting point within Dublin's political city centre.
stories: Ivy Day in the Committee Room

name: O'Connell Bridge
description: O'Connell Bridge is one of Dublin's central bridges connecting the north and south sides of the city. It appears in Counterparts and The Dead marking an important crossing point in Joyce's Dublin.

name: Phoenix Park
description: A 17th-century 707-hectare park created for royal hunting, mentioned as "the Park" and "the Parkgate" in the Dubliners.

name: Johnny Rush's
description: Johnny Rush's is a pub located on South William St. Historically it was a real business located near Findlater's place.

name: Bewley's
description: A cafe located on Grafton Street which was frequented by Irish literary and artistic figures, including James Joyce.

name: Theatre Royal
description: One of three major early 20th-century Dublin theatres, it was primarily used for dramatic presentations. It was demolished in 1962.

name: Port and Docks
description: Also known as Dublin Port, established in 1707 and headquartered at different places along the Liffey river.

name: Queen's Theatre
description: The Queen's Theatre was characteristically known as a venue for comic and musical acts. In 1969 the theatre was demolished.

name: Chapelizod Bridge Pub
description: It is the unnamed pub only distinguished in the story by its location at the Chapelizod bridge, with its connotations of romance and history, that offers Duffy the ability to feel tenderly.

name: Adam and Eve's
description: Adam and Eve's, the Franciscan church, was from 1889 officially the Church of the Immaculate Conception.

name: Ormond Quay
description: Ormond Quay is one of many waterfront spans of street lining the Liffey in central Dublin. Situated on the north bank, its upper and lower sections are bisected by Capel Street as it becomes Grattan Bridge.

name: Merrion Street
description: In Joyce's time Merrion Street was lined with Georgian townhouses built after Leinster House in 1748. Corley likely worked in one of these affluent homes.

name: Grattan Bridge
description: Grattan Bridge crosses the Liffey and joins Capel Street to Parliament Street. It was originally known as Essex Bridge before later rebuilds and renaming.

name: Dublin Castle
description: Situated in the heart of Dublin on Dame Street. A 13th-century structure built on Viking ruins, it served as the headquarters of British administration in Ireland for over 700 years.

name: Shannon Waves
description: Refers to the River Shannon located in the west of Ireland. It flows southward from the Cuilcagh Mountains in County Cavan to the Atlantic Ocean passing through 11 counties.

name: Tivoli Theatre
description: Formerly The Grand Lyric Hall / Lyric Theatre of Varieties, the Tivoli Theatre was located on Burgh Quay on the south bank of the Liffey. It was later acquired by the Irish Press newspaper group in 1930.

name: Kavanagh's
description: A spot for political under-the-table deals, located in Parliament Street between Essex and Dame Streets south of the Liffey.
stories: Ivy Day in the Committee Room

name: St Catherine's Church
description: St. Catherine's is a Roman Catholic church on Meath Street situated around the corner from Thomas Street.

name: Earl Street
description: Earl Street is located near the Liffey running between Sackville (O'Connell) Street and Marlborough Street.

name: Nassau Street
description: A prosperous shopping street running along Trinity College's southern boundary. Built on the former site of the 17th-century St. Patrick's Well Lane.

name: Kildare Street
description: Runs south from Nassau St. to the north side of St. Stephen's Green. Home to the exclusive Kildare Street Club.

name: The Kildare Street Club
description: An exclusive men's club in Joyce's time, predominantly Protestant and Anglo-Irish in membership, located at the corner of Nassau St. and Kildare St.

name: Hume Street
description: An elegant red-brick Georgian street running east from St. Stephen's Green approximately fifty yards from the northeast corner of the park.

name: Duke's Lawn
description: Also known as Leinster House, Duke's Lawn was the former ducal residence of the Duke of Leinster. In 1922 it was turned into the parliament building of the Irish Free State before it became the Republic of Ireland.

name: Capel Street
description: Capel Street is a shopping thoroughfare a few blocks from O'Connell Street. In the 17th century it was an integral part of Dublin's expansion north of the River Liffey.

name: City Hall
description: Built on Dame Street by the Guild of Merchants from 1769 to 1779. Before it became City Hall it was known as the Royal Exchange and was the epicentre of trade for Dublin's merchants.

name: George's Street
description: George's Street is home to the George's Street Arcade, one of Ireland's first purpose-built shopping centres built in 1881.

name: Westmoreland Street
description: Westmoreland Street links O'Connell Bridge and College Green in central Dublin. It is named after John Fane, 10th Earl of Westmorland, Lord Lieutenant of Ireland from 1789 to 1794.

name: College of Surgeons
description: The Royal College of Surgeons in Ireland (RCSI) was founded in 1784. It is a leading medical institution and the national body for surgery in Ireland, with its historic building located near St Stephen's Green.

name: Spring Gardens
description: Spring Garden is a northside area off North Strand Road sitting between the Royal Canal and the River Tolka. Today it survives as Spring Garden Lane. Joyce uses it as a social anchor placing Mrs. Mooney's butcher shop nearby to root her story in Dublin's working-class northside.

name: Belfry of George's Church
description: St George's Church is a notable historic church site located at Hardwicke Place in the north inner city. It was famous for its bells which serve as a potent audio presence in the life of the city in Joyce's novels.

name: Marlborough Street
description: Marlborough Street is a north inner city street running parallel and just east of O'Connell Street, best known as the location of St Mary's Pro-Cathedral.

name: North Wall
description: The North Wall area is historically a departure point closely associated with Dublin's docklands and emigration. It sits within the port-side zone running from the Custom House eastward toward Dublin Port along the north bank of the Liffey.

name: O'Neill's Shop
description: O'Neill's is a pub on Suffolk Street as part of the city-centre drinking network. It's where the character Farrington slips into "the dark snug of O'Neill's shop" for a glass of plain porter.

name: Fleet Street
description: Fleet Street sits within the central commercial core close to the Temple Bar and Dame Street corridors. It's where Joyce places Terry Kelly's Pawn Office anchoring Farrington's desperation in a real transactional part of the city.
